hero.lead: Costa Rica's boutique wellness hospitality industry has clear structural friction — and clear structural defenders. This Foundation analysis maps the forces, root causes, and boundary conditions that shape what's possible at the 5–12 villa scale.

chapters.label: The Industry
chapters.headline: A mature wellness hospitality market with three structural domains
body: Costa Rica's boutique wellness hospitality sits at the intersection of foreign-direct-investment-driven coastal development, hospitality operations under cantonal-municipal regulatory architecture, and global eco-luxury demand mediated through OTA platforms and incumbent brand consortia.

The Pacific corridor — Nosara, Santa Teresa, Manuel Antonio, Dominical, Uvita, Tamarindo — has matured into the country's most established wellness segment. National hotel performance has stabilized at approximately mid-60% occupancy through 2024, with growth concentrated in resort and higher-end Guanacaste segments. ICT has explicitly adopted a 'quality over quantity' growth philosophy with a deliberate 2–3% annual ceiling.

Five-to-twelve villa boutique resorts occupy a specific structural position: above the single-villa STR base on Airbnb-style platforms, below the hotel-scale and branded-resort tier, and fragmented across hundreds of independent operators with relatively few small chains and a small share of branded-boutique-group keys.

chapters.label: The Structural Forces
chapters.headline: Ten forces shape this industry — four regulatory, four commercial, two economic
body: On the regulatory side: cantonal fragmentation produces real timeline variance and a structural local-expertise premium; SETENA environmental viability and AyA water availability are hard preconditions, not soft gates; the Zona Marítimo Terrestre is constitutional public domain with no fee-simple ownership; Costa Rican labor and social-charge structure adds approximately 35% to nominal wages and constrains foreign expat staffing under the 90/10 rule.

On the commercial side: OTA distribution power and rate-parity dynamics structurally compress margin for new entrants; real-estate information asymmetry is maintained by absence of a centralized MLS; mature wellness markets are showing supply growth and ADR softening; three structural moat patterns held by incumbents — location, network embedding, and brand-consortia ties — make commoditization the default outcome for non-moated entrants.

On the economic side: capital scarcity at the 5–12 villa scale forces equity-heavy stacks of 50–80% sponsor capital because institutional debt and global hospitality lenders target larger tickets; climate, seismic, and water-stress exposure raise insurance, design, and operating costs.

chapters.label: Why It's This Way
chapters.headline: Four feedback loops sustain the friction
body: The structural forces converge on five root causes of friction. The most consequential is regulatory architecture fragmentation — cantonal authority over land-use, combined with independent national gates from ICT, MINAE/SETENA, AyA, and INVU, produces unpredictable timelines and a structural local-expertise premium that disadvantages outside developers. The second is the awkward financing position of 5–12 villa boutique — too small for institutional debt, too small for development finance institutions, forced into equity-heavy stacks. The third is the persistent information asymmetry that local intermediaries have structural incentive to maintain. The fourth is the commoditization risk faced by new entrants without one of the three incumbent moat patterns. The fifth is climate and water-stress gating in the most desirable Pacific dry corridor.

Four feedback loops explain why this friction persists. Cantonal fragmentation creates demand for intermediaries; intermediary economics raise the cost of regulatory navigation; foreign-developer dependence reduces external pressure to standardize. Saturation in core wellness markets compresses ADR; new-entrant exits stabilize ADR — markets self-correct via entrant exit, not demand expansion. Incumbent moats compound over time via repeat business, direct-booking share, and brand-consortia investment. Coastal development pressure raises water demand; aquifer stress triggers AyA denials; new development falls. Two reinforcing loops compound against new foreign entrants while two balancing loops constrain market and geographic opportunity.

chapters.label: The Question
chapters.headline: Is Costa Rica boutique wellness suited for this venture?
body: The Foundation gate question is whether we understand the structural forces, constraints, and dynamics of this industry well enough to identify real problems within it. The answer, on the strength of 464 citations and 10/10 investigation areas resolved, is yes.

The structural friction is real and well-evidenced. The defenders of the status quo have legitimate arguments — ICT's growth ceiling, mid-60% national occupancy stability, ADR softening signals — that any platform thesis must address. The boundary conditions are clear: ZMT public-domain structure, CR labor framework, climate exposure, territorial tax with 15% non-resident dividend WHT.

What the gate reviewer needs to decide: does Bhava's design-led, feminine-aesthetic, wellness-integrated boutique thesis fit one of the two viable new-entrant patterns — 'big and clear' (capex + brand) or 'small and sharp' (founder-led targeted retreat) — and avoid the commoditization fate of generic eco-chic? That question will be answered through Problem and Jobs research, not Foundation. Foundation says: the industry has structural friction worth investigating, and the analysis is evidenced enough to proceed.

cta.text: Scroll to Gate Review for the evidence briefing and decision options.

sections.headline: Ten forces shape this industry
sections.lead: Four regulatory forces, four commercial forces, two economic forces. Each is structural — industry-wide, not situational.

cards.title: SF1: Cantonal regulatory fragmentation
cards.body:
The same boutique resort project faces materially different checklists, sequencing, processing behavior, and
regulatory plans across Costa Rica's 82 cantons. Land-use confirmations, construction permits, operating
patentes, and ZMT regulatory plans are administered municipally; ICT (national tourism), MINAE/SETENA
(environmental), AyA (water), and INVU (urban planning) each gate independently with no unified portal or
standardized timelines.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Timeline variance and unpredictability for outside developers • Local-expertise premium captured by intermediaries • Reduced comparability of regulatory due-diligence across sites

cards.title: SF2: National environmental and water hard gates
cards.body:
SETENA environmental viability is legally preventive — projects cannot start without it — and AyA water
availability is defined as real and current capacity (not future or potential). These two gates structurally
precondition any boutique resort project. CGR audit shows meaningful operational risk inside the EIA system
(control inconsistencies, limited inspections, reliance on external consultants).
cards.extra_html: Creates: Hard preconditions on coastal/forested development • Local-expertise premium for navigating SETENA • Regional development gating in water-stressed Guanacaste

cards.title: SF3: Zona Marítimo Terrestre public-domain + concession structure
cards.body:
Ley 6043 (1977) defines the 200-meter coastal strip from mean high tide as public domain: 50m inalienable
Zona Pública + 150m Zona Restringida granted only via concession (administrative right, not fee-simple).
Concessions are typically 20-year terms, transferable only with municipal/ICT approval, and subject to
nationality conditions in some cantons. Special regimes (Polo Turístico Golfo de Papagayo, Ley 6758) allow
longer terms (up to 50 years for marinas) and centralize ICT authority.
cards.extra_html: Creates: No fee-simple ownership in maritime zone • Concession risk priced into coastal capital structures • Foreign-developer structuring complexity (Costa Rican-controlled entity often required)

cards.title: SF4: OTA distribution power and rate-parity dynamics
cards.body:
OTAs (Booking, Airbnb, Expedia) sustain power through take-rate economics (typically 10–25%, Airbnb single-fee
14–16%) and parity-style contracting (MFN-logic clauses limiting hotel ability to reward low-commission
channels with lower rates). Independent boutique CR operators have structural channel dependency, with a
large share of gross room revenue mediated by OTAs, especially in launch phase before direct-booking demand builds.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Margin compression for new boutique entrants • Direct-booking infrastructure premium for incumbents • Channel-dependence path that takes years to escape

cards.title: SF5: Real-estate information asymmetry
cards.body:
No centralized MLS exists in Costa Rica; listing data is fragmented across brokers and direct-to-owner
channels. Land pricing transparency, title verification, permit timelines, contractor pricing, and concession
eligibility are opaque to outsiders. Local intermediaries (agents, lawyers, brokers, consultants) hold
structural information advantage and capture premium for their access.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Local-expertise premium structurally maintained • Higher due-diligence cost for foreign-led projects • Persistent information advantage held by local intermediaries

cards.title: SF6: Capital scarcity at 5–12 villa boutique scale
cards.body:
The 5–12 villa scale falls into a structural financing gap: too small for institutional hospitality lenders
(global lenders Aareal, Berkadia, BHC focus on larger tickets), too small for crowd-sourced CRE platforms
(CrowdStreet, EquityMultiple), and too small for development-finance institutions (CABEI, IFC tilt to larger
tourism/infrastructure). Costa Rican commercial banks lend at conservative LTVs (40–60%) to foreign sponsors
with strong collateral and guarantees. Result: 50–80% of project cost is equity from sponsor balance sheet,
friends-and-family, and HNW co-investors or private syndicates.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Equity-heavy capital stacks (50–80% equity) at boutique scale • Slower scaling and limited capacity to expand by Phase Two • Sponsor-balance-sheet and HNW-network dependence

cards.title: SF7: Climate, seismic, and water-stress exposure
cards.body:
Coastal Costa Rica boutique hospitality faces material climate and seismic risk: hurricane intensity, sea-level
rise, erosion, earthquakes, and Guanacaste dry-season water scarcity. Aquifer overuse and salinization are
documented in Guanacaste; water conflicts with local communities are recurring. Climate risk is priced into
insurance availability and cost; seismic into structural design. SETENA increasingly scrutinizes climate-
sensitive sites.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Insurance pricing premium for coastal projects • Water-availability denial as structural development gate in dry corridor • Capital cost of resilience design

cards.title: SF8: Mature-market saturation signals + ICT growth ceiling
cards.body:
Established wellness/eco-luxury markets (Nosara, Santa Teresa, Manuel Antonio, Uvita, Tamarindo) show evidence
of supply growth combined with ADR/occupancy softening. National hotel performance has stabilized at mid-60%
occupancy with ADR softening through 2024–2025. ICT explicitly targets a "quality over quantity" growth
philosophy with a deliberate 2–3% annual growth ceiling, not aggressive expansion.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Margin compression for new boutique entrants in core markets • Strategic premium on under-served sub-segments and emerging frontiers • Pressure to differentiate beyond commodity boutique positioning

cards.title: SF9: Three structural moat patterns held by incumbents
cards.body:
CR boutique wellness incumbents defend margin via three structural moat patterns: (1) location moat —
grandfathered scarce coastal parcels (Pranamar, Florblanca, Olas Verdes), (2) network embedding —
yoga/retreat circuit ties producing predictable group bookings (Bodhi Tree, Blue Spirit), (3) brand/consortia
ties — international luxury network affiliation (Nantipa via SLH, Hacienda AltaGracia via Auberge, Virtuoso
memberships). New entrants without one of these moats face structural commoditization.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Two viable new-entrant strategies; generic boutique uncompetitive • Incumbent direct-booking share growth over time (compounding moat) • Premium on founder-led narrative or institutional brand affiliation

cards.title: SF10: Costa Rican labor and social-charge structure
cards.body:
CCSS (social security) combined contributions are roughly mid-30% of gross payroll (employer ~25%, employee ~10%);
INS workers' compensation adds risk-classified premium; MTSS minimum-wage decrees specify minimums by hospitality
role; aguinaldo (13th month), vacation, and severance are non-waivable. Ley 7064 establishes the 90/10 rule
(≥90% Costa Rican workforce, ≤10% foreign). Recent CCSS IVM contribution rate increases (2026) raise structural
payroll cost.
cards.extra_html: Creates: Material payroll-cost premium over nominal wages • Constraint on foreign expat staffing depth • Mandatory benefit-stack obligation regardless of operating performance

sections.headline: Five root causes
sections.lead: Where the structural forces converge to create friction. RC1, RC2, RC3 are addressable; RC4 is partially addressable through strategy; RC5 has a boundary-condition component.

cards.title:
Costa Rica's tourism regulatory architecture is fragmented across cantonal authorities and national environmental
gates,
cards.body:
Municipal authority over land-use + ICT (national tourism) + MINAE/SETENA (environmental viability) + AyA (water)
+ INVU (urban planning) each gate independently with their own portals, processes, and timelines. Per-cantonal
ZMT regulatory plans add another layer of variance. The combination produces unpredictable timelines and a
structural local-expertise premium that disadvantages outside (especially foreign) developers.

cards.title:
Boutique-scale (5–12 villa) hospitality occupies a structurally awkward financing position — too small for
institutional
cards.body:
Institutional hospitality lenders and global brand-backed financing require larger ticket sizes; CR commercial
banks lend at conservative LTVs to foreign sponsors with strong collateral and personal guarantees; CABEI/IFC
tilt to larger tourism/infrastructure deals; crowd-sourced CRE platforms have no consistent CR boutique pipeline.
Climate/seismic exposure adds a project-level risk premium that further deters debt providers. Saturation in
core markets compresses pro-forma underwriting margin. Net result: boutique scale is forced into all-equity
or HNW-syndicated capital, which limits scaling pace and exit liquidity.

cards.title:
Information asymmetry between foreign developers and the local Costa Rican market is structurally maintained
by the abse
cards.body:
Land prices, title histories, permit timelines, contractor pricing, and concession eligibility are mediated
through local intermediaries who hold private information advantage. Cantonal fragmentation amplifies the
complexity, making generalized self-research insufficient. The intermediary economy has structural incentive
to maintain opacity rather than democratize the information.

cards.title:
Mature wellness markets are reaching demand absorption limits while OTA structural concentration compresses
margin for n
cards.body:
OTA take rates (10–25%) plus rate-parity clauses limit pricing power for new entrants without direct-booking
demand. Saturation signals in Nosara/Santa Teresa/Uvita (supply growth + ADR softening) shrink the margin
buffer. Incumbent moats (grandfathered location, retreat-network embedding, brand affiliation) compound over
time via repeat business and direct-booking share. New boutique entrants without a moat face thin margins,
slow ramp, and exit pressure.

cards.title:
Climate and water-stress exposure structurally limits boutique expansion in Costa Rica's most desirable
Pacific dry corr
cards.body:
Guanacaste aquifer overuse and salinization combine with seasonal precipitation patterns to produce hard
water-availability gating via AyA. Hurricane and erosion risk raise insurance and design capex. SETENA
scrutiny of climate-sensitive sites is increasing. The cumulative effect is structural limitation on where
and how boutique can expand in the highest-demand wellness corridor.

sections.headline: Three complete chains from forces to friction
sections.lead: Each chain traces structural forces through intermediate causes to a root cause. Every link cites evidence and describes the mechanism.

cards.title: Chain CC1: 4 links
cards.body:
SF1 → per-canton permit-process variance (high confidence)
↓
SF2 → hard preconditions add timeline variance (high confidence)
↓
per-canton variance + hard-gate timeline variance → RC1 (high confidence)
↓
RC1 → foreign-developer disadvantage in time-to-permit, capital-time efficiency, deal access (high confidence)

cards.title: Chain CC2: 5 links
cards.body:
SF4 → new-entrant OTA channel dependence and margin compression (high confidence)
↓
SF8 → mature-market supply growth + ADR softening (high confidence)
↓
SF9 → incumbent moat compounding (high confidence)
↓
(channel dependence + saturation + moat compounding) → RC4 (high confidence)
↓
RC4 → high failure/exit rate among generic boutique entrants; only 'big and clear' or 'small and sharp' pa (high confidence)

cards.title: Chain CC3: 5 links
cards.body:
SF6 → 5–12 villa scale below institutional debt thresholds (high confidence)
↓
SF7 → climate/seismic risk pricing into insurance and design capex (high confidence)
↓
BC3 → hard environmental constraint on highest-demand corridors (medium confidence)
↓
(sub-threshold capital + risk pricing + environmental constraint) → RC2 (high confidence)
↓
RC2 → slow scaling, limited Phase Two capacity, thin private exit market (high confidence)

sections.headline: Four loops sustain the friction
sections.lead: Two reinforcing loops compound against new foreign entrants — local-intermediary economy and incumbent moat compounding. Two balancing loops constrain opportunity — saturation self-correction and climate-water 'limits to growth.'

cards.title: FL1: Cantonal regulatory fragmentation creates demand for local intermediaries...
cards.body:
Cantonal regulatory fragmentation creates demand for local intermediaries; intermediary fees and gatekeeping
raise the cost of regulatory navigation, which sustains foreign-developer dependence on intermediaries; that
dependence reduces external pressure to standardize cantonal processes; cantonal fragmentation persists.

cards.title: FL2: Saturation in core wellness markets compresses ADR...
cards.body:
Saturation in core wellness markets compresses ADR; new-entrant margin compression accelerates exits or
non-entry; supply absorption stabilizes; ADR partially recovers. Balancing dynamic — markets self-correct
via entrant exit, not via demand expansion.

cards.title: FL3: Incumbent location/network/brand moat → strong repeat business → growing direct-...
cards.body:
Incumbent location/network/brand moat → strong repeat business → growing direct-booking share → margin
growth → reinvestment in moat (capex, brand-consortia ties, marketing) → moat strengthens. Established
players compound their advantage over time; new entrants face widening gap unless they build a different moat.

cards.title: FL4: Coastal development pressure increases dry-season water demand...
cards.body:
Coastal development pressure increases dry-season water demand; aquifer stress rises; AyA water-availability
denials increase; new development rate falls; aquifer pressure stabilizes. Limits-to-Growth archetype —
growth engine encounters environmental constraint.

sections.headline: Four conditions a venture cannot change
sections.lead: The platform must work within these, not against them. Constitutional, statutory, and physical-environmental constraints.

cards.title: Zona Marítimo Terrestre (ZMT) public-domain + concession-only structure (Ley 6043, 1977)
cards.body:
Why immovable: Constitutional principle of public-domain coastal protection; statutory framework predates 50 years; courts and Contraloría have consistently nullified attempts to treat ZMT as private property. Reform unlikely to privatize the public band; tightening (not loosening) is the political direction.

Implications: No fee-simple ownership in the 200m maritime zone. Coastal boutique resorts must be inland of the 200m line or operate via concession (administrative right, not equity). Concession risk (revocation, non-renewal, transferability constraints, nationality conditions in some cantons) must be priced into capital structures. Special regimes (Papagayo) operate on different terms but don't change the underlying public-domain principle.

cards.title: Costa Rican labor and social-charge framework (CCSS, INS, MTSS, 90/10 rule)
cards.body:
Why immovable: Constitutional (Article 56, Article 74) and longstanding statutory framework; institutional structure deeply embedded; reform direction is incremental rate increases (CCSS IVM raised Jan 2026), not structural change.

Implications: ~35% combined social charges add materially to nominal wages. 90/10 rule (Ley 7064) constrains foreign expat staffing depth. Mandatory aguinaldo, vacation, and severance are non-waivable. Operating P&Ls must underwrite these costs from day one.

cards.title: Climate and water exposure of Pacific Costa Rica coastal corridor
cards.body:
Why immovable: Physical/environmental — ENSO precipitation cycles, dry-season patterns, sea-level rise trajectory, hurricane intensity trends. No venture intervention can change Pacific climate or aquifer recharge rates.

Implications: Boutique developments in Guanacaste/Nicoya must design for water positivity (rainwater capture, greywater recycling, on-site storage). Insurance and capex reflect climate risk premium. Site selection must consider erosion, flood, and storm exposure. Long-term horizon must price further climate degradation.

cards.title: Costa Rican territorial corporate income tax + 15% non-resident dividend WHT + 15% real-estate capital gains
cards.body:
Why immovable: Statutory framework (Ley del Impuesto sobre la Renta) with active reform debate but stable core structure. Limited treaty network — most foreign investors take direct hit on dividends. Reform direction is broader base / global income, not loosening.

Implications: Corporate income tax applies to CR-source revenue regardless of owner nationality. Dividend distributions to non-resident owners taxed at 15%. Capital gains on CR real estate at 15%. Tax structuring requires Costa Rican professional input; double-taxation mitigation is structurally limited.

sections.headline: Trends, disruption history, and counter-evidence
sections.lead: Where the structural landscape is shifting, what disruption attempts have shown, and the legitimate arguments for the status quo.

items.q: ICT 'quality over quantity' growth philosophy with deliberate 2–3% annual growth ceiling
items.a: Direction: Constraining new supply nationally; favoring premium/wellness positioning

items.q: Mature wellness markets (Nosara, Santa Teresa, Uvita) showing supply growth + ADR softening
items.a: Direction: Saturation signals in core markets; pressure to differentiate or migrate to emerging frontiers

items.q: Tightening RTBF beneficial-owner reporting enforcement (Ley 9416); CCSS contribution rate increases
items.a: Direction: Higher compliance and operating cost; greater transparency requirement on foreign-owned structures

items.q: Political pressure to tighten ZMT enforcement (Papagayo reform bill 2026)
items.a: Direction: Higher regulatory bar on coastal development; favoring fully-compliant operators

items.q: Climate-driven water stress in Guanacaste / Pacific dry corridor
items.a: Direction: Increasing AyA water-availability denials; pressure on water-positive design

items.q: Wellness/digital-nomad demand mix shift (longer stays, retreat formats, sober-curious)
items.a: Direction: Favoring properties with structured wellness programming and longer-stay infrastructure

Outcome: Capital-structure failure (CABEI loan acceleration / dispute, lender litigation)
Lesson: Capital-structure experiments (condo-hotel presales + debt) are non-restartable once lenders accelerate, even on mostly-built projects

Outcome: Multi-year stall pending court/agency resolution
Lesson: Entitlement and environmental compliance disputes can stop projects for years; SETENA + community veto are real

items.q: Generic eco-chic small boutique entrants in core wellness markets
items.a:
Outcome: Commoditization, slow ramp, exits or quiet repositioning
Lesson: Boutique without a structural moat (location, network, brand) drifts into OTA-mediated commodity rates

Outcome: Mixed; capital-structure fragility a recurring failure mode
Lesson: Fractional and residence-club models work where governance and operating infrastructure are strong; fail where presales-financed and lender-dependent

items.q: ICT explicitly targets 'quality over quantity' with 2–3% annual growth ceiling, suggesting the segment is mature, healthy, and deliberately not under-built
National hotel performance has stabilized at mid-60% occupancy with supply growth absorbed; the existing landscape adequately serves wellness/eco-luxury demand
items.a: Source: EY Law / Chambers / STR national data

items.q: ADR softening in Nosara, Santa Teresa, Uvita signals that core wellness markets may not absorb additional new supply without further price erosion
5–12 villa scale is structurally awkward — too big to be owner-operated, too small to gain operating leverage or attract brand affiliation
Climate, water, and political risks (RTBF tightening, ZMT reform pressure) favor incumbents with scale, political capital, and brand power over small foreign-led entrants

sections.headline: Six points of consensus, four points of disagreement
sections.lead: Where experts agree about the industry's structural challenges, and where they diverge. Disagreement signals uncertainty and reveals where the landscape is not fully understood.

cards.title: Cantonal regulatory fragmentation produces real timeline variance and a structural local-expertise premium
cards.body: Sources: CGR (Comptroller General), Costa Rican law firms (BLP, Arias, EY Law, BDO)

cards.title: SETENA environmental viability and AyA water availability are hard preconditions, not soft gates
cards.body: Sources: CGR audit, MINAE primary regulatory text, Multiple Costa Rican legal commentaries

cards.title: The 5–12 villa boutique scale is equity-heavy financed with limited debt access for foreign sponsors
cards.body: Sources: HVS Latin America, JLL Hotels, Hospitality finance commentaries

cards.title: Three structural moat patterns — location, network embedding, brand-consortia ties — defend margin among CR boutique inc
cards.body: Sources: Skift, Hospitality Net, Industry interviews

cards.title: Climate and water exposure are material project-level risks for coastal CR hospitality
cards.body: Sources: Climate-risk publications focused on Central America, Insurance industry publications, CR environmental research

cards.title: ICT 'quality over quantity' philosophy and 2–3% growth ceiling are real institutional commitments
cards.body: Sources: ICT national tourism authority, CR tourism academic research

cards.title: Whether the 5–12 villa scale is structurally weak or workable
cards.body:
• Structurally weak — too big to be owner-operated, too small for brand affiliation or operating leverage
• Workable as 'small and sharp' founder-led targeted retreat with strong local integration

cards.title: Empirics of Tulum-to-Costa Rica wellness traveler migration
cards.body:
• Real and substantial — anecdotal evidence from operators, social commentary, OTA share-of-search shifts (proprietary)
• Overstated — no public quantitative dataset measures defection; demand may be additive rather than migrating

cards.title: Whether ADR softening in Nosara/Santa Teresa is cyclical or structural saturation
cards.body:
• Cyclical — post-COVID demand normalization combined with currency effects, not structural
• Structural saturation — supply growth has outrun demand absorption in mature wellness towns

cards.title: Whether ICT 2–3% growth cap is a binding institutional commitment or aspirational framing
cards.body:
• Binding — ICT actively constrains supply via permit pressure and 'quality over quantity'
• Aspirational — actual growth is determined by SETENA, AyA, and municipal permit issuance, which collectively respond to market demand
cards.extra_html: Sources: ICT statements, Counter-commentary from industry analysts

sections.headline: Foundation gate decision
sections.lead: The gate question and the evidence briefing. Validation passed all 7 criteria.

data.question: Do we understand the structural forces, constraints, and dynamics of this industry well enough to identify real problems within it?
strengths: 10/10 investigation areas resolved with strong evidence after 2 research rounds
464 citations across regulatory, commercial, technological, capital, demand, and competitive dimensions
99% of sources from 2024+ — well above 50% recency threshold
Multiple structural forces converge on a small set of root causes via causal chains supported by multi-source evidence
Counter-evidence actively sought; legitimate status-quo arguments addressed proportionally
gaps: Quantitative ADR/occupancy time series by named wellness town (paywalled STR/AirDNA — inherent public-source limit)
Empirical Tulum→CR migration measurement (proprietary OTA share-of-search data)
Property-level financials (RevPAR, GOPPAR, margins) for named CR boutique incumbents (not publicly disclosed)
Statistically rigorous insurance cost benchmarks for foreign-owned 5–12 villa coastal operations

validation.text: Root causes identified — 5 root causes with termination tests
Causal chain evidenced — 3 chains, all links cited

decisions.label: PROCEED
decisions.title: Industry has structural friction worth addressing
decisions.body: Advance to Problem workspace. The industry shows clear structural friction (cantonal fragmentation, capital scale gap, information asymmetry, commoditization risk for non-moated entrants, climate gating). The boundary conditions are documented and bounded. Counter-evidence does not undermine the case for investigating; it sharpens what the venture must overcome. Problem workspace will need stakeholder transcripts to validate practitioner pain.

decisions.label: REVISE
decisions.title: Landscape needs deeper investigation
decisions.body: Re-run Foundation research with refined investigation areas. Specific gaps to revisit might include: empirical migration data, property-level financial benchmarks, or alternative geographic frontiers (Caribbean coast, Osa peninsula). Cost: another research round (~$2–3).

decisions.label: KILL
decisions.title: Industry doesn't fit a venture thesis
decisions.body: Stop. The structural picture suggests one of: ICT's 'quality over quantity' ceiling combined with mature-market saturation makes new boutique entry structurally unattractive; the 5–12 villa scale is structurally awkward and unworkable; or the boundary conditions (climate, ZMT, labor) make the unit economics non-viable.

sections.lead: Deduplicated across both research rounds. 99% from 2024+. Organized by category.
